one_line_takeaway: A federated recommender that lets each user pick which attributes to hide and strips exactly those from their representation via shared per-attribute mutual-information filters, beating LDP/noise on the privacy-utility tradeoff while barely touching non-private users' recommendations.
key_finding: On MovieLens with FedGNN as base, UC-FedRec cuts gender inference AUC from 0.817 to 0.631 and age micro-F1 from 0.489 to 0.283 while NDCG@10 only drops 0.843->0.783 and Recall stays 0.544->0.509. In the strong-protection regime (beta=0.8) gender AUC falls to 0.5 (random) while still retaining HR@10=0.75 and NDCG=0.457 (89.0% and 84.0% of base), whereas the LDP and Gaussian-noise baselines retain only ~72% and ~50% of utility at comparable privacy. At the same similar-utility point, UC-FedRec protects attributes better than simple early stopping. Non-private users keep 96.4% HR / 97.0% NDCG (beta=0.7, Douban), and convergence speed is similar to the base FedRec.
core_contribution: UC-FedRec, a user-consented federated recommender framework that defends against attribute inference attacks while letting each user individually choose which of their attributes to protect. Its novel component is a set of compositional 'sensitive attribute filters' (per-attribute distribution estimators) that sit on top of an arbitrary embedding-based FedRec base model: users who deem an attribute non-private help train that attribute's estimator (cross-entropy), while users who deem it private apply the shared estimator adversarially to strip that attribute's information from their learned representation. A per-user trade-off weight beta lets each client set their own utility-vs-privacy balance, and a 'central protection' mode lets the server retroactively protect a newly-sensitive attribute by updating only that user's embedding layer, without a new federated round. The authors bill it as (to their knowledge) the first user-consented / personalized-and-compositional privacy FedRec.
threat_model: Honest-but-curious setting where an adversary trains attribute classifiers (neural networks) on the FedRec-learned user representations to infer users' hidden sensitive attributes (e.g., gender, age, occupation, location). The attack is empowered when some participants voluntarily reveal their attributes: because collaborative filtering ties together behaviorally-similar users, the attacker can propagate from disclosed attributes to infer the sensitive attributes of users who never revealed them. The paper explicitly does NOT defend against a malicious server (stated in Ethical Considerations); gradient transmission is separately protected by 1-LDP (Laplace noise), and the filters target inference from the learned model itself, which the authors argue standard DP does not address.
limitations: Explicitly assumes a non-malicious server (Ethical Considerations note shared info could face greater risk under a malicious server). Protection quality depends on how many users volunteer attributes: as the private-attribute ratio alpha rises from 0.3 to 1, utility at a fixed protection level drops (HR 0.75 -> 0.63 at AUC~0.56) because estimators are less well-trained; at alpha=1 it degenerates to LDP-like noise protection. For simplicity all private attributes are weighted equally per user. The defense is adversarial/discriminative and requires knowing/enumerating the attributes to protect; the authors flag non-adversarial, unsupervised extension to unseen attributes as future work. Only two datasets and two base models are evaluated, and protection is imperfect (residual leakage remains above the random-attacker floor for most settings).

datasets_used: MovieLens (1,000,209 ratings, 6,040 users, 3,952 items; gender/age/occupation treated as private attributes)
Douban (647,263 interactions, 6,368 users, 22,347 items; location treated as private attribute)
quantitative_results: MovieLens/FedGNN: NDCG@10 0.843->0.783, Recall 0.544->0.509 under UC-FedRec (utility cost)
MovieLens/FedGNN attribute inference under UC-FedRec: gender AUC 0.817->0.631, age micro-F1 0.489->0.283, occupation micro-F1 0.171->0.128
MovieLens/FedNCF under UC-FedRec: NDCG 0.784->0.734, gender AUC 0.755->0.602, age micro-F1 0.358->0.212, occupation micro-F1 0.152->0.117
Douban/FedGNN: NDCG 0.724->0.688, location micro-F1 0.265->0.221; Douban/FedNCF location 0.263->0.225
At beta=0.8 gender AUC=0.5 (random) with HR@10=0.75 (89.0% of base) and NDCG=0.457 (84.0% of base); LDP/noise baselines retain only ~72%/~50% of utility
Non-private users (beta=0.7, Douban): HR@10 0.698 and NDCG 0.477 = 96.4%/97.0% of base
Number of private attributes (MovieLens): protecting all attributes still yields HR 0.773 / NDCG 0.479 = 91.7% / 88.3% of non-private users
Central protection: MovieLens age micro-F1 0.387->0.291 with HR 0.829->0.768; Douban location micro-F1 0.304->0.265 with HR 0.721->0.698
Personalized single-attribute filters: age filter drops age micro-F1 to 0.313 vs 0.489 base, more precise than gender filter's 0.444
Motivating Table 1 (existing FedRecs on MovieLens): Fedfast-BPR gender AUC 0.745 / NDCG 0.72; LDP-Rec gender AUC 0.685 / NDCG 0.694 vs random-attacker floor 0.5 gender, 0.141 age, 0.05 occupation
baselines_compared: FedNCF (federated neural collaborative filtering base model)
Early stopping (of the base FedRec, at matched recall)
Gaussian noise perturbation on trained private user embeddings (sigma = 1,3,5,7)
LDP: Laplace noise on client gradients (privacy budget epsilon = 0.1,0.3,0.5,0.7)
